The Therapeutic Process in the Treatment of Schizophrenia11

Edward Hoedemaker M.D.

Edward Hoedemaker

Edward Hoedemaker  

SUMMARY

Certain observations have been presented in the intensive treatment of a schizophrenic patient which have to do with the oral incorporative process in the therapeutic method. Implications have been drawn regarding the probable pathogenic process in schizophrenia, and the reparative therapeutic process has been described.

responseSet.title: Re-Evaluation of the Libido Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Re-Evaluation of the Libido Theory11

Norman Reider M.D.

Norman Reider

Norman Reider  

Robert Waelder, Chairman, opened the panel by remarking that this was the first in a proposed series of discussions on the libido theory and that the three main presentations introduced three different types of approach to the theory.

In order to locate the principal papers in the general framework of the libido theory, an attempt is herein made to arrange systematically, though not at all completely, in outline form, a breakdown of the theory into its component parts, indicating their relation, more or less, to some other areas of psychoanalytic theory.

Basic theoretic concepts inherent in the libido theory:

1. Necessity of the theory as a prolegomenon to metapsychology

2. Necessity of the theory as an operational concept

3. Qualitative aspects

a. relation to physical and chemical energy

b. relation to biologic processes and needs

c. relation to a general theory of instincts

d. relation to a general theory of psychic function

1.

Recent British Approaches to Problems of Early Mental Development11

Elizabeth Zetzel M.D.

Elizabeth Zetzel

Elizabeth Zetzel  

In a well-known critical paper published several years ago, Edward Bibring2 discussed the theories of the "so-called English School." It is not entirely clear however, how far the term "so-called" refers to his evaluation of the analytic thought and how far to the national origin of its authors. Although all three books discussed in this review were published in England, only one of the six authors concerned may properly be described as English. With the exception, moreover, of Fairbairn's very individual Scottish approach, the views expressed did not originally develop in the British Isles. The fact, however, that two of these controversial volumes have been published in the International Psycho-Analytical Library, edited by Ernest Jones and published by the Institute of Psycho-Analysis, reflects one very important characteristic of British psychoanalysis—namely, its capacity to contain within its existing framework controversial and possibly divergent views and their proponents. Certain hypotheses expressed in these volumes might well have led to overt schisms in the United States. This point is particularly well illustrated by Balint's work, which derives from some of Ferenczi's controversial propositions. In this country Ferenczi's pupils, as is well known, have developed modifications both of theory and technique, which have actually led to the development of new groups. Balint, in contrast, remains active in the scientific life of the London Institute of Psycho-Analysis.

1Melanie Klein, Paula Heimann, Susan Isaacs, Joan Riviere, DEVELOPMENTS IN PSYCHO-ANALYSIS. London: Hogarth Press, 1952, 368 pages, $5.50.

Michael Balint, PRIMARY LOVE AND PSYCHO-ANALYTIC TECHNIQUE. London: Hogarth Press, 1952, 288 pages, $5.50.

Ronald D. Fairbairn, PSYCHO-ANALYTICAL STUDIES OF THE PERSONALITY. London: Tavistock Press, 1952, 312 pages, $5.00.

2Edward Bibring, The so-called English school of psycho-analysis. Psychoanal. Quart., 16, 1947.534

[[RunningHead]]

Christmas "Neurosis"11

L. Boyer M.D.

L. Boyer

L. Boyer  

SUMMARY

5 Case material derived from patients who were interviewed in psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy and psychoanalysis revealed that their Christmas depressions were the result of unresolved sibling rivalries. In them the birth of Christ, a fantasied competitor against whom they were unable successfully to contest, reawakened memories of unsuccessful rivalry with siblings, real or fantasied, in their pasts. Oral conflicts were stimulated, and repressed cravings and frustrations were rearoused. They sought uniformly to obtain penes with which they imagined they could woo their mothers to give them the love which they felt had been previously unequally showered upon their siblings. There was some indication that they at times identified with Christ in an attempt to deny their own inferiority and to obtain the favoritism which would be His just due.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—Validation of Psychoanalytic Theory11

Henry Brosin M.D.

Henry Brosin

Henry Brosin  

The chairman opened the meeting with the announcement that discussion from the floor was invited in order to have an open conference rather than a limited symposium. This resulted in twenty-one lively discussions of the five principal papers. The extensive material does not permit an easy summary of even the major presentations, much less of the subsidiary topics. It is regretable that all the topics offered cannot be included, but it is nonetheless hoped that the truncation necessary for a brief report will not result in gross distortion.

In an introduction which served as a keynote paper for many of the important issues under discussion, Roy R. Grinker described the work of a psychoanalyst as a participation in a complex two-way process in which there are direct and indirect influences, even though the analyst attempts to remain a neutral observer, detached in space behind a screen. In this highly active process, the first-hand observations about motivations, needs, and satisfactions cannot be separated from conceptual models. He cited an example from the recent literature in which ideas inherent in the theories of libido and infantile sexuality were closely associated with the language of clinical data, clinical associations and hypotheses, all in one paragraph. There is the further difficulty of separating the metapsychological problems (theories about theories) from the simpler theories which are more directly bound to observations.

The fact that the analyst is usually a physician with therapeutic responsibilities and ambitions as well as a person with research goals adds further complications. Grinker mentioned the trials experienced by his group in setting up an experiment to study the nature of aggression.

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting, New York, December, 1954. Chairman: Roy R. Grinker, M.D.489

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Dualistic Viewpoint on Anxiety11

Joachim Flescher M.D.

Joachim Flescher

Joachim Flescher  

Before we attempt to re-examine the second anxiety theory of Freud, it would be wise to recall what compelled him to abandon his first one. He believed that the concept of a direct transformation of libido into anxiety could not be sustained, as the unpleasurable nature of the anxiety affect was contradictory to libidinal discharge. That anxiety is not due to a release of libidinal energy was, according to Freud, proven by the situation in conversion hysteria where the libidinal cathexes of the repressed impulses are still retained in repression. The intimate connection between libido and anxiety had to be loosened also, because of the clinical fact that repression of impulses that are not libidinal in nature can arouse anxiety. Furthermore, Freud had to account for the observation that anxiety appears prior to repression. In addition, the role which previous experiences play in the anxiety situation had to be clarified. Finally, he had to take into consideration the structural differentiation of the mental apparatus then recently introduced. The answer to the question of the localization in and participation of a specific psychic structure in the anxiety phenomenon became unavoidable.

It is well known that in Freud's new formulation, the ego was held responsible for anxiety. The economic origin of anxiety became, as he reiterated, irrelevant because, according to his insight at that stage, the ego was working with desexualized energy.

And yet in the second formulation some of the concepts of his earlier one have been retained, in so far as Freud distinguished automatic anxiety from signal anxiety: he considered the first one "economically justified, " that is, caused by an onslaught of stimuli

1Read before the New York Psychoanalytic Society, December, 1953.2"We like to conceive of the ego as powerless against the id, but when the ego struggles against an instinctual force in the id, it merely needs to give a signal of distress to attain its purpose through the aid of the all but omnipotent pleasure principle" (12, p. 18).

responseSet.title: Bisexuality in Symbolism
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bisexuality in Symbolism11

Samuel Guttman M.D.

Samuel Guttman

Samuel Guttman  

Freud (2) states that "symbolism is not peculiar to dreams but is characteristic of unconscious ideation, in particular among the people, and it is to be found in folklore, and in popular myths, legends, linguistic idioms, proverbial wisdom and current jokes, to a more complete extent than in dreams" (p. 351). The dreamer employs symbols to disguise latent thoughts and our knowledge of the marked plasticity of physical material makes it worth while considering whether a symbol, from the viewpoint of psychoanalytic theory and observation, can ever be assumed to be exclusively male or female.

The purpose of this brief communication is to examine the following proposition: Can bisexuality be inherent in any symbol?

In this communication symbolism is considered in its strict sense—a representative of the primary process (Jones, 7). The symbol is more concrete, shorter and more condensed than the ideas it represents. Such representation of affect or thought is primitive both phylogenetically and ontogenetically and depicts a simpler and earlier stage of development. Rank and Sachs (10) conceive of a symbol as "a special kind of indirect representation which is distinguished by certain peculiarities from other allied kinds, such as the simile, the metaphor, the allegory, the allusion and other forms of figurative representation of thought material and it is a substitute, illustrative replacement—expression for something hidden."

Stekel (16) first propounded the generalization of bisexuality in symbolism.

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December, 1953.280

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Problem in Psychoanalytic Technique

Frederic Worden M.D.

Frederic Worden

Frederic Worden  

The clinical material for this report is based on three and a half years of analytic work (approximately 750 hours) with a young woman. For the first two and a half years, progress was slow and the analysis was very stormy. During the last year, the analyst acquired a clearer understanding of one aspect of the material, namely, the relationship between unconscious guilt and certain disturbances in ego function of the patient. This understanding resulted in a more consistent and systematic interpretative technique and the patient responded by making dramatic and unexpected progress.

This clinical experience raises a number of questions in the psychoanalytic theories of ego function and technique, and illustrates some problems in relating theory to practice. In the first place, the patient falls into the group which has been called "the borderline states." There was marked invasion of secondary process by primary process, with impaired reality testing, and a restriction in the capacity to postpone gratification of impulses so that the ego's observing and integrating functions were jeopardized. Knight (6), discussing some therapeutic considerations regarding "borderline states, " describes how, in the analytic situation, these patients will bring forth misleading "rich" material but that often the analytic interpretations of it cannot be utilized by the patient's ego. He says, "For the sake of accurate diagnosis, realistic prognosis, and appropriate therapy, therefore, the clinician must be able to locate the position, movements, and possibilities of synthesis of the main ego forces and functions, and not be misled by all the shooting in the forward holding point.

[[RunningHead]]

Setting a Termination Date—an Impetus to Analysis11

Martin Orens M.D.

Martin Orens

Martin Orens  

SUMMARY

A patient was presented who came into analysis with a postpartum depression of one year's duration. In the course of the analysis it was found that her most pressing problems had to do with the female castration complex. During analysis she had a second child with no depression. She requested discharge and was given a definite termination date. The threat of separation from the analysis caused a flow of previously unknown material, and deeper levels of the unconscious were uncovered. The importance

664

of finding out the meaning of the analytic situation and the end of the analysis was stressed.

responseSet.title: Childhood Phobias
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Childhood Phobias11

Anne Benjamin M.D.

Anne Benjamin

Anne Benjamin  

George E. Gardner opened the Panel on Childhood Phobias with a few remarks about its history. Previously the Panel had been concerned with Schizophrenia in Childhood. Now it had been decided to focus on the Neuroses beginning with Childhood Phobias.

The main presentation was given by Peter Neubauer. The questions posed in his paper were based on experiences at the Council Child Development Center. Here children are studied and treated in their prelatency years in connection with a therapeutically oriented nursery school. The paper focused on early disturbances in children which might be considered as preneurotic manifestations of phobic development.

The signs of disturbance in the first two years which seem most related to the mechanism of phobic conflicts are: excessive screaming, reactions of displeasure in the feeding situation, sleeping disturbances, frequent reactions to noises, and a history of no disturbance in toilet training. Case material was presented from the literature to point out the similarities in the early histories of phobic children.

These signs are not considered exclusively as evidence of preneurotic manifestations of future phobic symptoms. There may be constitutional disturbances as seen in the "atypical child" whose ego never gains the strength to neutralize or defend against libidinal and aggressive drives and who responds to external stimuli as dangerous. Organic pathology may disturb ego function by changes in perceptual experience.

These signs may also be observed as reactions to external disappointments and frustrations. The instinctual needs of the infant are frustrated by "inappropriateness of the mother's attitude" so that pain is experienced instead of pleasure.

1Presented at the Annual Meeting, St. Louis, May, 1954, Chairman: George E. Gardner, M.D.309

[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Early Infancy11

George Gardner M.D.

George Gardner

George Gardner  

In his introductory remarks John Benjamin gave a brief systematic survey of various areas of psychoanalytic research which differed in methods as well as in goals. He stressed the need for testing and expanding the genetic propositions of psychoanalysis by psychoanalytically illuminated longitudinal observations of infants and children. The present panel, he stated, would deal with the restricted but highly important and necessary area of what can and what cannot be meaningfully predicted from very early neonatal observations.

In two papers entitled "Neonates" and "The Same Children in the Second Year, " Katherine M. Wolf presented material from a current research program that is being carried out at the Child Study Center at Yale University. In general these are longitudinal studies on child behavior beginning at the prenatal stage. The specific aspect which she presented was the observation of the neonate with the view, if possible, of delineating forms or patterns of behavior in the individual child at birth or shortly after which would have more or less predictive value in respect to the child's later responses and personality development.

Wolf opened her discussion of individuals at birth by emphasizing that she was not seeking the answer to the old controversy of constitution vs. environment, though naturally speculation about the relative importance of these factors was inevitable by the very nature of the age level with which she was working and the type of behavioral observations that were being made. She indicated that she and her co-workers differed from Fries' contributions because they did not feel that selection of differences in motor behavior offered the most promise for the establishment of reliable predictive measures. She felt that differences in activity were fated to be largely descriptive and felt it was probably false to assume that similar behavior or different levels of development meant the same thing.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Avenues of Approach to the Schizophrenic Patient11

Leo Stone M.D.

Leo Stone

Leo Stone  

While psychoanalysis was invoked very early in its history for the understanding of psychoses, its therapeutic application to these disorders was thought impracticable because of the psychotic's narcissistic incapacity for transference. However, since Abraham (1), who originally shared this attitude toward schizophrenia (1908), published his work on the manic-depressive psychosis (2), efforts to psychoanalyze psychotics, especially schizophrenics, have become increasingly frequent. A considerable group of writings on this subject deals with these efforts in relation to schizophrenics, or (more often) with less strictly defined psychotherapies in which psychoanalytic ideas play an important part. (See 13), (14), (4), (19) as examples of early and recent reviews.) It merits special statement that Abraham himself, in 1916 (3), mentioned the analysis of a case of schizophrenia simplex.

Thus the first obstacle to be overcome was the inhibiting pessimism regarding therapeutic contact with schizophrenics; soon thereafter came the published demonstration of actual and effective transference relationships with a certain number of schizophrenic patients. However, the apparent mastery of the "sine qua non" in certain cases left us with other no less formidable problems: Can such relationships be established with large numbers of severe cases, and if so, how? What are the essential nature, meaning, and remote effects of these relationships?

1John N. Rosen, DIRECT ANALYSIS: SELECTED PAPERS. New York: Grune & Stratton, 1953, 192 pages, $3.75.

Book Notices

An Object-Relations Theory of the Personality, by W. Ronald D. Fairbairn. New York: Basic Books, 1954, 312 Pp., $5.00.

A collection of papers with a brief preface by Ernest Jones. Most of the articles were previously published in British medical journals and in the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. The major portion of the book, consisting of seven papers, attempts a reformulation of the libido theory and of Abraham's "phase" theory of ego development; the author's formulation is presented in terms of the ego's relationship with objects, particularly those "internalized during early life." Three papers are devoted to clinical problems. The final section contains miscellaneous papers originally intended for nonpsychoanalytic audiences, including a paper on sexual offenders and a discussion of the sociology of communism.

AN OUTLINE OF A COMPARATIVE PATHOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES, BY Ludwig Eidelberg. New York: International Universities Press, 1954, 264 pp., $4.50.

Addressed to advanced students of psychoanalysis, this book presents a condensation of classic psychoanalytic theory and practice, with certain addenda and modifications by the author within this framework, together with a comparative, "quantitative" approach to neurotic symptoms, character traits and perversions. In this quantitative appraisal, the relative intensity of libidinal and aggressive drives are assayed for each type of clinical phenomenon. The question of tolerance toward unpleasure in neurotics, and its implications in therapy are discussed. The quantitative approach is also a focus of the discussion on libidinal types. A detailed description of various attitudes of the total personality to the pathological end results of defense mechanisms is presented, together with an attempt to assay the quantitative factors involved in terms of the intensity of the unpleasure caused by the narcissistic mortifications involved. The choice of neurosis is discussed from the standpoint of Freud's concept of the libidinal types. The author suggests study of the "cross-sections of neurosis" in which the end result of the defense mechanisms is examined from the point of view of the id, ego, superego, and external world.

responseSet.title: Forepleasure: Its Use for Defensive Purposes
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Forepleasure: Its Use for Defensive Purposes11

Ralph Greenson M.D.

Ralph Greenson

Ralph Greenson  

SUMMARY

I have tried to demonstrate that, although forepleasure is primarily devoted to the various pregenital instinctual components, zones and aims, it is also frequently used for important defensive purposes. Forepleasure offers an excellent opportunity to study the direct results of the mechanisms of defense and also many of the ego's more complex defensive measures in action. It is particularly striking how frequently people use forepleasure activities to enact and act out a fantasy which has the purpose of denying or counteracting some frightening aspect of sexuality. Indulging in fantasies seems to have the over-all purpose of distracting the individual from the sexual act in which he is engaged. There seems to be an antagonistic relationship between the amount of fantasy in forepleasure and the object relationship. The greater the amount of fantasy, the less the object and the self is cathected in forepleasure. All of these considerations seem to point to the fact that forepleasure is often not just pleasure but also a serious matter.

responseSet.title: Gogol—a Study on Wit and Paranoia
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gogol—a Study on Wit and Paranoia11

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer  

SUMMARY

1. Wit and paranoia offer related and alternative forms of discharge for certain repressed drives as well as social tendencies. Consequently wit may prove of symptomatic significance in relation to paranoia as well as to incipient social protest.

2. The interplay between a paranoid individual and a discontented social group is traced in the career of Gogol. Repressed individual and political forces found outlets in common symbols which permitted each to find representation and discharge through the other.

3. Freud's description of the wit work is consequently amended. The elaboration of the preconscious by the unconscious required for this process can take place either directly or with the group acting as intermediary ("object passage"). The relaxation of group inhibitions through jests secondarily affects the censorship within the individual. The "brother horde" is enlisted to reinforce the ego in its comic or real battle with the father figure.

4. The multidetermination of symbols may lead to unconscious misinterpretation of audience approval, so that serious impairment of reality testing results. Even in psychosis, however, the disavowed individual may truly represent the real wishes and the future trends developing within the general population.

[[RunningHead]]

Sullivan's Interpersonal Theory of Psychiatry11

Edith Jacobson M.D.

Edith Jacobson

Edith Jacobson  

This book represents Sullivan's latest conception of psychiatry. It is composed of a series of lectures given in 1946 and 1947 which have been edited by H. S. Perry and M. L. Gawel, with Mabel B. Cohen as psychiatric consultant.

In her introduction, Cohen gives a concise, brief outline of Sullivan's scientific development and of his most important contributions to psychiatry. She points to his absorbing interest in obsessional and schizophrenic manifestations and in "the specific patterns of interaction" between people which Freud presumably neglected in his earlier writings. Despite her emphasis on Sullivan's psychoanalytic training, Cohen admits Sullivan's failure to take into consideration phenomena such as infantile sexuality and hysteria. As we shall see, these remarks describe Sullivan's attitude to psychoanalysis very benevolently.

The first, most important, part of the volume presents "Introductory Concepts." The next 264 out of 384 pages are devoted to discussions of "The Developmental Epochs." The third part deals with mental disorders, i.e., schizophrenic manifestations, and the brief last part offers suggestions for "a psychiatry of peoples."

As always, Sullivan's diction and style are most characteristic and inimitable. His rather bizarre mixture of naïve-popular and complicated-formalistic thinking and writing is colorful, amusing, and—to me—quite fascinating. Occasionally, though, his longwinded sentences remind one of German philosophers—except for the lack of profundity evident in them.

To begin with the question of nomenclature, Sullivan confesses that he does "not like to coin fresh terms." But Freudian terms must have been anathema to him. Apparently quite unaware of the significant distinction between unconscious, preconscious, and conscious, he replaces these "old-fashioned" terms by "overt" and "covert"; he rejects

Book Notices

BEETHOVEN AND HIS NEPHEW, by Editha and Richard Sterba, translated by Willard R. Trask. New York: Pantheon Books, 1954, 352 pp., $5.00.

Disclaiming any "intention of making a psychological study of Beethoven's works or of his creative gift or activity, " the authors attempt to reach the psychological truth concerning the behavior of this fascinating human being. This "truth" consists essentially of the story of the tragic consequences of Beethoven's pathological love for his nephew Karl, which was the chief expression of his unsuccessful attempts to reconcile "the polarity between the male and female principle" in his personality.

BEYOND THE GERM THEORY. THE ROLES OF DEPRIVATION AND STRESS IN HEALTH AND DISEASE, edited by Iago Galdston. New York: Health Education Council, 1954, 182 pp., $4.00.

An extension, revision and integration of material presented at the 12th Annual Eastern States Health Education Conference sponsored by the New York Academy of Medicine. Eleven papers emphasize the disease-producing effects of deprivation and stress, and are divided into four parts, "The Dynamics of Deprivation and Stress, " "Nutritional Deprivation and Stress, " "Psychological Deprivation and Stress, " and "Social Stress and Deprivation." The material of the book centers around the concepts of Claude Bernard ("the internal milieu"), Walter Cannon ("homeostasis"), and Hans J. Selye ("the stress syndrome"). Psychoanalytic contributors are René A. Spitz, "Unhappy and Fatal Outcomes of Emotional Deprivation and Stress in Infancy"; and Abram Kardiner, "The Emotional Effects of Social Stress and Deprivation, I and II."

DANIEL PAUL SCHREBER. MEMOIRS OF MY NERVOUS ILLNESS, translated and edited with introduction, notes and discussion by Ida Macalpine and Richard A. Hunter. London: William Dawson and Sons, Ltd., 1955, 416 pp., £1, 15s.

This book makes available in English translation for the first time the classic memoirs of Schreber.

responseSet.title: A Contribution to the Problem of Termination of Training Analysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Contribution to the Problem of Termination of Training Analysis

Therese Benedek M.D.

Therese Benedek

Therese Benedek  

The problems related to the termination of training analysis are determined, just as in any other process nearing its end, by problems related to its beginning and to its course. We cannot hope for clarification of the problems concerning its termination unless we can clarify the function of the personal analysis of the future psychoanalyst in the total psychoanalytic training process.

As long as training in psychoanalysis was acquired in a manner of apprenticeship (1), the interrelation of the three aspects of training—personal analysis, supervised analyses, and theoretical courses—and their effective co-ordination could not be studied since in each instance the interpersonal relationship between teacher and pupil dominated the training. Since Ferenczi (5) stated that training analysis needs to be more thorough than therapeutic analysis, the time required for training analysis, which originally had been of but few months' duration, has become longer and longer. One reason for this is that the goal of the psychoanalytic process has become increasingly complex with the evolution of psychoanalysis as a science and a therapeutic tool. The other reason, however, is rather doubtful because it originates in the expectation that if only the personal analysis is carried on long enough, all problems, which in a given case might interfere with the successful progress of a candidate, could be resolved and the goal of his personal development as well as his training would be achieved. Yet such optimism needs scrutiny beyond the clinical evidence in reference to the various phases of training, since there are indications that the effectiveness of the training analysis might be seriously impeded by the interference of the training process itself.

615

responseSet.title: On Obsessive Thoughts
responseSet.authorMast: George Gero and  David L. Rubinfine
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Obsessive Thoughts11

George Gero Ph.D. and David Rubinfine M.D.

David Rubinfine

David Rubinfine  

Among our patients we not infrequently meet women who are tortured by an obsessive fear of killing a child. This idea occurs in men too, but it is probably commoner in women. Some of the women have one or more children of their own; others have none and never had any. Those with children are usually good mothers, genuinely fond of their child. It is therefore all the more terrifying for them to feel a strange impulse, expressed as a wish to harm, to injure or even kill their child. The urge and the fear in these women is not necessarily limited to their own children. The sight of a baby carriage or of children playing in the park can elicit the obsessive fear. Such an obsessive fear is ordinarily but one symptom in a clinical picture, for the women are ridden with other obsessive symptoms.

In this case report of a woman patient, the obsessive thought to kill a child was but one of the many obsessive thoughts which tortured her and which led to a severe narrowing of her participation in normal human activities.

We begin this report with an inquiry into the mechanism of the obsessive fear to kill a child. The reason for this choice is the fact that this symptom is paradigmatic for the underlying mechanism of other obsessive symptoms in the patient.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Problems of Termination of the Training Analysis

Emanuel Windholz M.D.

Emanuel Windholz

Emanuel Windholz  

The evaluation of the problems related to the termination of the didactic analysis can be successful only to the degree to which we are able to understand its role in the total process of training. A great deal of disagreement could be eliminated if we could arrive at a clarification of the theoretical concepts upon which we operate. Some are taken for granted, others are implied, and many others cannot be defined at this stage of our knowledge.

THEORETICAL CONCEPTS

One of our basic assumptions is that the didactic analysis is indispensable as a preparation for the profession of the psychoanalyst (1), (2), (11), (12), (14). In his analysis the candidate will resolve his own emotional conflicts, which will enable him to observe objectively the unconscious of his patients. In order to accomplish this the didactic analysis has to be therapeutically effective. But there are important differences between a didactic and a therapeutic analysis. In terms of goals, we expect from the didactic analysis more than relief from neurotic suffering. If our patients develop tendencies to analyze others we consider these as remnants of their transference and attempt to analyze them further before termination. However, these same attitudes are desirable in the candidate: he should identify with the "professional activity" of his analyst.

In the didactic analysis the candidate acquires via transference the analyst's skills. This type of learning is an essential part of every didactic analysis, though we do not "teach" any theoretical concepts.

641

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Anxiety—A Statement of a Unitary Theory11

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

To the psychiatric clinician, anxiety is the raison d'etre. The total network of its tortuous course, its origins, direct manifestations, its equivalents, derivatives, and consequences (defenses, symptoms), constitute the major background, and indeed the foreground of the clinician's field of operation. This paper is thus focused on a central, albeit a diffuse phenomenon. It will have proven a justified effort, even without the expectation of any new development of theory, if it serves merely to refresh and recapitulate, and to bring a subject as nuclear as this periodically up to date.

The statement or warning that if one starts at any point of the psychoanalytic framework one can proceed to touch on every other part is particularly applicable to a study of anxiety. The challenge it poses, besides that of precise understanding, is clearly one of selection and condensation. This presentation will attempt to serve as a bridge between the historical developments of the past (51), and the experimental findings and paths for the future, which are to follow (32). The areas to be examined will include: (a) an appraisal of the present theoretical status of anxiety and its place in psychoanalytic metapsychology, the divergences of opinion which exist, and a synthesis of these into a unitary theory; (b) the integration of the concept of anxiety into the psychoanalytic theory of affects and into the most recent developments in ego psychology; (c) some remarks on the role of anxiety in the clinical therapeutic process.

1Read at the Symposium on Anxiety, Section on Psychoanalysis of the American Psychiatric Association, St. Louis, May, 1954.389

[[RunningHead]]

Dependency Processes in the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenia11

Harold Searles M.D.

Harold Searles

Harold Searles  

SUMMARY

This paper has been concerned with dependency processes in the psychotherapy of schizophrenia. It describes what I consider to be the major sources of the patient's anxiety about his dependency needs; the major unconscious defenses which the patient utilizes, within the context of his relationship with the therapist, to ward off awareness of these needs and of the anxiety associated with them; and indicators of the presence of anxiety in the therapist with regard to such needs in the patient and, basically, in himself. The paper concludes with a number of points in regard to pertinent therapeutic technique.

[[RunningHead]]

The Reconstruction of a Traumatic Childhood Event in a Case of Derealization11

Victor Rosen M.D.

Victor Rosen

Victor Rosen  

There has been considerable revision and elaboration of the role played by a particular childhood experience in the dynamics of an adult neurosis since the first publication of the Studies in Hysteria(2). The development of Freud's thinking on this subject (3), (4), (5) and the direction of later revisions is clearly indicated in the discussion of fixation upon traumas in the Introductory Lectures(6). In this context Freud comments: "Everything said today relates only to symptom formation in hysteria. Even the obsessional neurosis shows great differences, although the essentials are the same. … Similar and more extensive deviations still are found in the other neuroses, in which field researches into the mechanisms of symptom formation are not yet complete in any direction."

The following case of the reconstruction of a severe childhood trauma at about the age of three and a half years, in a patient suffering from the symptoms of derealization, bears directly upon this problem. It emphasizes the role of identification as another mechanism for the resolution of the traumatic experience, and re-emphasizes the position of the reconstruction of the "traumatic fixation" in the advance of both theory and therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

Intuitive Processes in the Psychotherapy of Schizophrenics—Introduction11

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann M.D.

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann  

The papers presented in this panel are the outcome of one set of investigations in this research project.2

The papers are based on the following procedure: Five staff members of the Chestnut Lodge Sanitarium got together for recorded weekly conferences of two hours' duration for a period of nine months. We listened to a part of one of the continuously recorded interviews which one of us had made with a schizophrenic patient whom we all knew. In conference we compared our reactions to the recorded material and to our own comments, in an effort to detect the sources of variations in our reactions, as they related to our differing life histories, personalities, life experiences, clinical experiences and relationships with the reporter. The study of this part of the project is still under investigation.

In our efforts to delineate the factors which promote or inhibit the analyst's intuitive awareness, it became clear that many of these factors have been grouped under headings other than intuition per se.

1Presented at the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 5, 1953. Dr. Marvin L. Adland's paper, "Countertransference and the Intuitive Processes of the Psychoanalyst, " presented at this panel, is not being published with this group of papers.2This research was started under the auspices of the Social Research Fund, Inc. and has been continued under the auspices of its successor, The Foundation's Fund for Research in Psychiatry.5

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Reports—The Borderline Case11

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell  

This panel, gathered to discuss the characteristics and problems of the borderline case, turned at least in its first half largely into a discussion as to whether "borderline states" existed at all.

The opening presentation was a paper by Gregory Zilboorg on "Clinical and Therapeutic Issues in So-Called Borderline Cases." Referring first to the previous definitive paper on the subject by Robert Knight two years ago, Zilboorg pointed out that selecting this subject for a panel discussion again so soon indicated a sense of incompleteness in which "we seem to seek we know not yet quite clearly what." The so-called borderline state is actually a misnomer, which Knight also felt, since he put the term in quotation marks. The trouble seems to be that we automatically think in the older Hippocratic or Kraepelinian terms, seeking for specific neurotic or psychotic mechanisms to denote specific clinical pictures, without having questioned the correctness of psychiatric nosology to begin with. The only real attempt to produce a new nosology which would represent a gradual and almost imperceptible pathological series of transitions was made by Adolph Meyer, but unfortunately this was weighted down by his static conceptions and unwieldy terminology. The major contributions toward a solution of the nosological problem were to be made by psychoanalysis, which introduced the novel concepts of typical psychological mechanisms, typical stages of libido development, and typical ego variants.

1Presented at the Annual Meeting, St. Louis, May, 1954, Chairman: Ralph R. Greenson, M.D.285

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toilet Symbols and Fantasies

Mortimer Ostow M.D.

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow  

It is the purpose of this communication to call attention to the fact that allusions to the toilet apparatus occur in certain, frequently encountered, stereotyped forms in dreams, waking fantasies, and in the unconscious fantasies which determine neurotic behavior. These stereotyped allusions may be called symbols for they are similar in their properties to the well-known symbols of anatomical structures, subjects and objects of instincts and technique of instinctual gratification as we recognize them in the derivatives of unconscious fantasies.1 Some of these symbols will be presented together with the instinctual techniques with which they are associated.

CLINICAL DATA

Case A. In the analysis of patient A, a young lady, a fantasy of being inside a toilet bowl recurred frequently, in many different versions, and with different affective implications. She reacted with disgust to the fantasy of being contaminated and soiled by other people whom she regarded as turds. She reacted with anxiety to the fantasy of her mother's attacking her by defecating on her. There were fantasies of being fed by the mother's feces, both as positive yearnings and in defensive form associated with the feeling of disgust or the fear of being smothered (by anal equivalent of the breast). She was uncomfortable in dirty places and equally uncomfortable in places that were shiny, clean, white and sanitary.

1However, if universal occurrence and racially archaic origin are considered necessary for determination of symbols, then the allusions to be discussed here must be called only representations.682

[[RunningHead]]

Special Problems in Connection with Termination of Training Analyses11

Edith Weigert M.D.

Edith Weigert

Edith Weigert  

Therese Benedek presented the opening paper of the panel. She described the development of training from a rather short apprenticeship to an increasingly complex process. She doubted whether the growing length of training analyses achieved the desired goal and joined in the warnings of Balint and Glover against an all too dogmatic and hierarchic training organization which could interfere with the creative scientific independence of the trainee. She pointed out that countertransference of the training analyst may be affected by the group psychology of the training system, and quoted Freud's recommendation: that training analysis has accomplished its purpose if it imparts a sincere conviction of the existence of the unconscious and a first sample of the analytic technique. Thereafter, Freud said: "We hope that the processes of Ego transformation will go on of their own accord and that the trainee will bring his new insight to bear upon all his subsequent experiences." Benedek sees us confronted today with the challenge "to guide the course of psychoanalytic training between the Scylla of superficial and inadequate personal analysis and the Charybdis of interminable training analysis in order to avoid the introjection of all too rigid scientific ideals."

Training analysis is different from therapeutic analysis in its goals. It "opens up the personality to the process of interminable analysis, which is the fate as well as the equipment of the psychoanalyst." Anna Freud has pointed out that the patient in therapeutic analysis has an unobstructed way to project his fantasies onto his analyst, while for the patient in training this projection is obstructed by the analyst's realistic significance for the future of his candidate patient.

1Presented at the Annual Meeting, St. Louis, May, 1954, Chairman: George J. Mohr, M.D.314

responseSet.title: On the Meaning of Agoraphilia
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Meaning of Agoraphilia

I. Glauber M.D.

I. Glauber

I. Glauber  

In the course of delving into the etiologic roots of the stuttering syndrome, and other oral-narcissistic syndromes to which it always led, I was impressed by the central importance of the psychological symbiosis between mother and child and especially by the many consequences of its incomplete dissolution. The point of fixation to which regression occurs later on is an early stage of ego organization. It is approximately that which has been variously designated: "pleasure ego" by Freud (4), "mouth ego" by Hoffer (10), "part-ego" by Lewin (13). Glover (8) states that it is the period when the development of speech leads to the organization of the preconscious layers of the mind. The functioning of this early evolving ego state of the child, with its boundaries indistinct and still confluent with the mother, is by means of a sense of identity with her omnipotent total self or with parts of her body, especially breast or fantasied phallus. This functioning is archaic and preoedipal and has to be distinguished from the later true identifications emerging after resolution of the oedipus complex and even from their precursors which Freud called "primary identification" (5). The distinction between the primitive and genuine identification is that the former strives to retain an archaic identity while the latter signifies a wish to assume, in the future, a new role—that of a love object; also, that in the former we are often dealing with an identity with a part-object while in the latter with an assumption of roles of a total object; finally, that the former state is beset with ambivalent cathexes while the latter strives to be unambivalent.

These remarks are germane because this paper is about one aspect of the incomplete dissolution of the primitive identification.

Termination of the Training Analysis Within the Framework of Present-Day Institutes

Rudolf Ekstein Ph.D.

Rudolf Ekstein

Rudolf Ekstein  

In a recent survey "On Current Trends in Psychoanalytic Training" (11) I recalled Freud's reference (17) to educating, healing, and governing as the three impossible professions, and suggested that the training analyst has chosen all three. For, he analyzes the students, he teaches, and he participates in decisions concerning the student's fate. The complex interweaving of these functions, which he must carry directly or indirectly, offers a seemingly "impossible" task within the context of the training analysis, particularly during the termination phase when the decision has to be made as to whether the requirement of the training analysis has been fulfilled. This requirement, which is a keystone in psychoanalytic training, has been established for about thirty years.

Even before 1922, at which time the standards were formally established, most of the early analysts underwent training analyses. While there is general acceptance today that every analyst must undergo an analytic experience of his own, widely divergent opinions exist regarding the nature of the training analysis, its timing and place in the total training, and the special technical problems it creates. The Selection of Candidates for Psychoanalytic Training: On the Use of Interviews and Psychological Tests11

Robert Holt Ph.D. and Lester Luborsky Ph.D.

Lester Luborsky

Lester Luborsky  

It is quite obvious that psychoanalysts are going to be selected for psychoanalytic training by methods that rely more or less heavily on interviewing. The interview is the most widely used technique in all kinds of personnel selection, and certainly is the mainstay of most psychoanalytic Education Committees. No matter what evidence might turn up that interviewing was unreliable, deceptive, or what not, most of us would still probably insist on interviewing a prospective secretary before hiring her. Which is to say that this is partly a rational but also partly a nonrational matter; it is deeply entrenched in tradition and habit, and our job therefore is to make the best of it.

Let us approach the task of improving the interview by considering some of the weak points of the interviewing in the selection project carried out at the Menninger School of Psychiatry. Perhaps if we get a better understanding of the results obtained in our research, we may be able to see how to do better.

To begin with, it cannot easily be maintained that the results were no better because we did not have good men to do the interviewing.4 The

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy11

Joseph Chassell M.D.

Joseph Chassell

Joseph Chassell  

Last year this panel attempted to approach the concrete problem of how the choice of specific psychotherapy in individual cases is determined. We discussed the thesis that in actual fact the patient tries to make the therapist meet his conception of his needs, and that when real movement takes place the therapist does enter the needed reciprocal role, but continues it in a productive new direction. The therapist is thus the second variable in the equation; hence, the topic of the dynamics of his participation.

But to study the patient is relatively easy—it is our daily job—while to study the therapist is ticklish business, and tends to be systematically slighted in case presentations. The writers hesitate to be too self-revelatory of their own feelings and involvements and are fearful of being regarded as too "dramatic" according to the canons of sober scientific papers if they give the true flavor of the actual interchange in the therapeutic relationship. Yet, as Adelaide M. Johnson summarized in her introductory remarks, "What can we do better than to expose the living bilateral treatment experience to our colleagues, to everyone's eternal benefit? … Everyone of us knows that 70 per cent of the therapeutic operation never appears in most of our papers." Not merely "countertransference" problems, but the whole dynamic of the therapist's participation was our ambitious goal.

Sydney G. Margolin undertook the task of delineating the general background of the psychology of the psychotherapist, working both from the available literature and from conversations with about forty analytic colleagues. (These data were not statistically treated.) He utilized the metapsychological format, looking first at the genetic and dynamic factors in the therapist's choice of his role. He noted the general view that the same psychology characterizes the patient and the therapist, the essential factor distinguishing the therapist being his ego

[[RunningHead]]

Hypnoid State, Repression, Abreaction and Recollection

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans Loewald  

It was the investigation of hysteria which led Freud toward the fundamental discoveries of psychoanalysis. In the following remarks I propose to re-examine some of Freud's early ideas concerning the mechanisms of hysterical phenomena, in an attempt to make explicit certain insights, implied in his earlier writings, which seem to have been blurred subsequently. In particular, I wish to re-examine the early conception of the hypnoid state and its relation to repression.

It appears that Freud, under the impact of his discovery of the basic phenomenon of defense, abandoned the concept of hypnoid hysteria, formulated in accordance with Breuer's ideas about hysteria. In the paper "On the Psychical Mechanism of Hysterical Phenomena" (1893) Freud says, concerning the hypnoid state: "… among the exciting factors of hysterical symptoms we also discover under hypnosis ideas which … owe their preservation to the circumstance that they originated during a period of seriously disabling affect, as for example, terror, or of a clearly abnormal mental condition, such as the half-hypnotic twilight state of day-dreaming, auto-hypnosis, and the like. In these cases it is the nature of these conditions which made a reaction to the event impossible." In other words, this "group of conditions is not determined by the content of the recollections, but by the mental condition of the patient at the moment when the given experience occurred" (1, p. 33).1

1The translation has been slightly modified.201

Sublimation11

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow  

An attempt to reformulate the problem of sublimation in terms of more recent advances in psychoanalytic theory, especially ego psychology, constituted the main endeavor of this panel.

In his introductory paper, the chairman of the panel, Heinz Hartmann, reviewed the history of the concept of sublimation. Originally, it was devised as a conceptual tool in a specific frame of reference, namely, conflict over the discharge of sexual drives. In time, the term, sublimation, acquired new and broader meanings in different contexts in psychoanalytic theory. As a result, present-day usage of the term is often confusing and ambiguous. In the beginning, Freud defined sublimation as a means of achieving instinctual libidinal discharge, and, at the same time, avoiding the necessity for repression through a displacement of the aim and/or the object of the drives onto activities of significant cultural value. Objections to this definition have come from many quarters, and, according to Hartmann, these objections may be subsumed under four general headings.

1. First, clinical analytic material seems to indicate that it is not completely true that sublimation involves exclusively a substitution of the aims and the objects of the sexual drives.

2. Those definitions of sublimation which stress the displacement of the sexual drives from manifestly instinctual aims to aims of a higher order of social estimation obviously introduce an element of value judgment. Such valuation is a highly questionable procedure in a scientific investigation of mental processes; it is certainly logically untenable in the elucidation of such problems as the relationship between sublimation and the development or creation of value systems.

3. A definition of sublimation, moreover, should consider the element of stability of ego functions, i.e., the resistance of certain functions of the ego to regressive transformation and sexualization. Resistance

[[RunningHead]]

Presidential Address: Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association11

Ives Hendrick M.D.

Ives Hendrick

Ives Hendrick, M.D.
Boston

Ives Hendrick  

I

It is my pleasure and honor to address you today, twenty-five years since I enjoyed the inspiring experience of becoming a young and eager member of the American Psychoanalytic Association, and of joining my sixty-five fellow members at the Midwinter Meeting in New York in 1930. During these twenty-five years I have shared with my contemporaries in our profession an almost unique experience of participation in the development of a new field of scientific inquiry which could legitimately be thought of by most observers a quarter century ago as of dubious objectivity. Within so short a span of years—far fewer than it takes to traverse one's professional maturity—psychoanalysis has become a profession of many members, widely recognized to have some validity and value, and, indeed, to be a necessity for the scientific advancement of some fields of modern medicine. Gazing retrospectively, I cannot see that any criterion existed whereby I, or my predecessors in psychoanalysis, could have disproved those numerous critics who, familiar with our literature but not with our clinical observations, sometimes thought of us as crackpots, or emotional converts, or merely victims of perverted imagination, in 1930. One can only say, the events of a quarter of a century have proved that our youthful conviction in studying analysis was based upon true perception of its value as a useful tool of psychological exploration.

In 1955 we are an association of over 600 members. But this impressive number is today only a fraction of those responsible for analytic science, therapy, and education, if we consider also that candidates registered for training in fourteen Institutes and three Training Centers accredited by us recently numbered 925 physicians

1Annual Meeting, American Psychoanalytic Association, May 8, 1955.2Dr. Harry I. Weinstock, Chairman, Central Fact-Gathering Committee of the American Psychoanalytic Association, which is engaged in a statistical study of psychoanalytic therapy, reports that, as of April 1, 1955, members and candidates had reported 3, 700 cases currently in therapy by psychoanalysis (and 3, 200 in psychotherapy) (3). As only 65 per cent of the members, and slightly over 70 per cent of the candidates, are participating in the return of questionnaires for this study, and not all participants had reported on April 1, 1955, his estimate of over 5, 000 patients certainly, and probably nearly 6, 000, in analysis by analysts and candidates accredited by this Association seems reliable.

Validation of Psychoanalytic Techniques11

Judd Marmor M.D.

Judd Marmor

Judd Marmor  

The panel discussion was opened by a presentation on "The Validation of Psychoanalytic Interpretations" by the panel chairman, Charles Brenner. The essence of his presentation was as follows:

The analyst forms a conjecture about his patient, presents this conjecture to the patient as an interpretation at the technically appropriate time, and looks for either confirmation or refutation of his interpretation in the patient's response. The analyst's conjecture is usually formed in an intuitive way, but the steps should be retraceable consciously.

The acid test of a conjecture comes after it has been offered to the patient as an interpretation, and is furnished by the response of the patient. In general the response to a correct interpretation is a lessening of the ego's fear of the id, a relaxation of the ego's defenses, and the emergence of a derivative of the id impulse that was being defended against. Such a response is considered to be confirmatory of an interpretation.

The response may be immediate or delayed. It may be divided into a subjective and an objective part. The former is what the patient reports, the latter is what we can observe. Typical confirmatory responses would include: (1) diminution of anxiety, (2) symptomatic improvement, or its opposite in a case where there was a predominant need to suffer, (3) a confirmatory memory, fantasy, dream or other verbal association, or a confirmatory gesture, all with or without an appropriate emotional experience, (4) a feeling of surprise, recognition, or 'that clicks' on the part of the patient, (5) sudden laughter.

Other types of confirmatory evidence which are not dependent on a lessening of the ego's fear of the id are: (1) repetition of what was interpreted, (2) confirmation of a reconstruction from an external source, (3) ability of a reconstruction to enable the analyst to fit together

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Significance of Intuitive Processes of the Psychoanalyst

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann M.D.

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann

Frieda Fromm-Reichmann  

Three of the preceding presentations offered are concerned with the clinical significance of the analyst's interrelatedness with schizophrenics and, implicitly or explicitly, with its influence on the promotion or inhibition of his intuitive processes. I will restrict myself, therefore, to the discussion of only one clinical example from my own experience, and use this is a starting point for a short review of our initial findings about the analyst's intuition as they transpire from the papers presented.

Many years ago, I described this experience. I had worked with a patient for a long period of time. Despite her assaultiveness a good mutual working relationship had been established. One day, while she was in a wet pack, I asked her whether she would be willing to give a legally needed signature. Her prompt answer was, "if you unpack me." Turning away from the patient I walked toward the door of her room to go and ask the nurses to do so. On the verge of leaving the room I looked back at the patient without realizing why. I believed at the time, that as I looked back at her I caught the clue from the expression of disappointment and frustration, if not despair, on the patient's face, that "if you unpack me" meant me, and not the nurses. I came back and opened the pack singlehandedly, and she gave me her signature. Later on he patient related this experience as having been the first step toward her recovery.

responseSet.title: The Ego, the Body, and Pain
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ego, the Body, and Pain11

Thomas Szasz M.D.

Thomas Szasz

Thomas Szasz  

SUMMARY

With the exception of Freud's "pleasure-pain principle, " the phenomenon of pain has received very little attention in psychoanalysis. An attempt is made in this paper to approach the problem of pain with the conceptual tools of modern ego psychology and to integrate it into the framework of psychoanalytic theory.

The intimate theoretical linkage between the concept of the ego and the nature of affects is noted. Examination of the psychoanalytic model of the mental apparatus suggests that this model is so constructed that an understanding of the problem of pain is greatly hindered. This assertion is based predominantly on the ambiguous position which the body (of the self) occupies in classical theory. An extension of our views is proposed in line with the fundamental notion of object relationships. It is suggested (1) that we conceive of the ego as relating not only to internal and external objects, but also to the body of the self, as an object; and (2) that we look upon pain (Schmerz) as an affect, having to do with the relationship of the ego to the body. Evidence is presented to justify this view and to demonstrate its value.

Pain as an affect, warning the ego of possible injury or loss of the body, is a construct analogous to anxiety as a signal warning the ego of the danger of (interpersonal) object loss. This "meaning" of pain applies primarily to those parts of the body which can be visualized (cf. the body image). The conception of disruption of structure and loss are thus readily associated with the covering of the body (the skin), appendages and other parts of the body, and functions, such as vision, hearing, etc., which may also be "lost." In addition, pain may constitute a reaction to and a warning against the danger of excessive stimulation. This meaning of pain applies to those parts of the body which are within the outer covering, at some depth from the surface.

It follows from the close connection between the ego and affect that developmental changes in the ego must reflect themselves in parallel changes in the nature of pain and anxiety. We conclude

197

that in the earliest phase of the ego, when there is only a differentiation between ego and non-ego, but no separation among different objects, there is no pain (Schmerz) or anxiety, in the adult sense of these terms. The affect pertaining to this stage of development is best described as undifferentiated pain-anxiety (or Unlust). At a later stage of development, when the infant can differentiate between its own body and the mother, the primitive affect of Unlust can be said to evolve into the feelings of pain and anxiety. The former reflects the ego's orientation to the body, the latter to objects (persons). With maturation the affects of pain and anxiety acquire many overdetermined meanings. These are influenced by the family environment, illness, and by the cultural setting. The affects of pain and anxiety may also appear as defenses, each against the other.

Psychoanalytic ego psychology is anchored in the notion of the ego's defenses against anxiety. The ego's defenses against pain have not heretofore been considered in psychoanalysis. Indeed, this whole area of enquiry is customarily thought of as belonging to organic medicine. These considerations lead to the problem of so-called "organic, " "psychogenic, " and "experimental" pain. In the framework of concepts presented in this paper, the terms "organic" and "psychogenic" are without meaning. Their relevance is retained, however, not as descriptions of the pain experience, but as judgments of an observer. The difference between "organic" and "psychogenic" pain is thus shown to be similar to that between "realistic" fear and "neurotic" anxiety.

In conclusion, a brief review of, and commentary on, the relevant psychoanalytic literature is appended.

[[RunningHead]]

The "Intuitive Process" and its Relation to Work with Schizophrenics

Alberta Szalita-Pemow M.D.

Alberta Szalita-Pemow

Alberta Szalita-Pemow  

The word "intuition" has been in use for some time now to fill in a gap whenever it is apparently not possible to explain in other ways how a certain body of knowledge has been obtained. At first, philosophers used this term to indicate a self-evident truth. Even as late as the time of Descartes, the word was used with this meaning. Descartes defined intuition as an indubitable, self-evident truth; i.e., a truth such that, in order to understand it, one would need only to know the meaning of the words used in expressing it. As frequently happens with words, the term "intuition" eventually took on a meaning opposite to the original one. Bergson (1) defined intuition as instinct at its best. "By intuition, " he said, "I mean instinct that has become disinterested, self-conscious, capable of reflecting upon its object and of enlarging it indefinitely." Lately, the term has been in use in psychiatry and other professions to indicate the unexplainable, unaccountable, instinctive, extrasensual, supernatural, or magical type of knowledge.

The word "intuition" stems from the Latin word intueor, which means: (1) to look at, upon or toward anything with attention or minutely; (2) to look upon or at (mentally), to observe, reflect upon, consider, contemplate; (3) to look at anybody with amazement; (4) to have respect for, pay attention to, keep before the eyes, weigh over, think upon. (Latin-English Dictionary, by Freund-Bullions, New York, 1870.)

Now let us examine how these words are used in colloquial speech.

7

responseSet.title: The Concept of Anxiety in Relation to the Development of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Anxiety in Relation to the Development of Psychoanalysis11

Elizabeth Zetzel M.D.

Elizabeth Zetzel

Elizabeth Zetzel  

SUMMARY

I have attempted in this paper to review briefly the psychoanalytic concept of anxiety, tracing some of the landmarks in the development of Freud's views on the subject. I have also tried to indicate the trends of recent developments, attempting as far as

386

possible to limit my attention to specifically psychoanalytic contributions. From the period of Freud's earliest discoveries anxiety has occupied a central position in psychoanalytic theory. In the first place, here more than anywhere else, we are on the borderlines between the realms of psychology and physiology. This has been evident throughout, from Freud's earliest formulations of the anxiety neurosis as a "toxic" manifestation to recent stimulating work on the physiological and psychological effects of unresolved sustained stress particularly in the early months of life. That anxiety bears some relation to physiological stress appears to be undoubted. In addition, however, it appears that this same physiological stress is at a relatively early stage of development experienced by the psychic apparatus. Here it is localized in the ego, or its more primitive precursors, and serves as a stimulus for the development of defensive processes. The quality and efficiency of these defenses will at every period of life be determined partly by the greater or lesser demands which have been put on the organism in the earliest stages of development, partly on the degree to which a new danger situation whether external or internal regressively revives older fears, partly on the degree and extent of the new stimulus, partly on inborn factors possibly related to the capacity to tolerate unrelieved tension without somatic discharge. In spite of the very great importance for future development of recent work in related fields, the detailed understanding of the predisposition to anxiety, of specific anxiety situations, and of the various methods by which they are handled, has been and remains the main task of clinical psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Problems in Communication with Schizophrenic Patients

Donald Burnham M.D.

Donald Burnham

Donald Burnham  

SUMMARY

I have discussed some of the problems of communication with schizophrenic patients, with particular reference to one patient with whom my efforts to facilitate communication were initially hampered by my assumption of total responsibility for understanding the verbal content of his communications. With the aid of sound recordings and a study seminar, my concept of my therapeutic task changed from that of providing expert translation to that of helping the patient assume his share of responsibility for successful communication. My view of the obscurity of the patient's communications changed from one vaguely describing his language as jumbled or crazy to one recognizing various meanings of the obscurity as a result and index of the patient's anxiety, a means of concealing frightening feelings and a compromise between the patient's intense desire for and equally intense fear of an extraordinarily intimate relationship. I then found myself less bound to a search for a single meaning of the verbal content, and the patient's communications took on an expanded range of meaning for me. I was more alert to cues such as tone, rhythm, volume of voice, and body movements. Noting the timing and context of verbal obscurity was often more useful than efforts to translate the obscure content. His uses of talk for direct pleasure, relief of loneliness, "filler" to sustain our relationship and a means of dramatizing conflicting self-concepts were often revealed more by the form of his speech than its content. Meanwhile, the verbal content itself also became less obscure to me. A number of factors contributing to the obscure content were delineated, including: the use of concrete verbal imagery instead of abstract symbolic terms, inability to focus thoughts and speech and maintain their organization into separate categories, need for verbal self-instructions, inability to discriminate between essential and minor details,

80

and the perception of different Gestalten. The elucidation of some of the attitudes and concepts which facilitated my better understanding of the patient's communications furnished a more explicit statement of what might otherwise have been lumped under the rubric of increased intuitive awareness.

The Basic Fear11

Gert Heilbrunn M.D.

Gert Heilbrunn

Gert Heilbrunn  

SUMMARY

The presence of anxiety preparedness in the newborn is ascribed to his inherited fear of annihilation. Phylogenetic and human ancestral reflections in conjunction with psychoanalytic data point to the ever-existing threat of passive cannibalistic incorporation as the basic danger. In defensive need its cathexis is shifted to the castration complex, which assumes the character of exuvial sacrifice. Its significance is treated in the light of Freud's theory of anxiety and the emphasis which the British School places on the child's cannibalistic projections.

[[RunningHead]]

Special Problems in Connection with Termination of Training Analyses

Edith Weigert M.D.

Edith Weigert

Edith Weigert  

I would like to start with two quotations from Freud to Ferenczi in the Fall of 1909, recently reported by Balint (2). They are probably some of the earliest remarks on training analysis. "Eitingon is here, goes twice weekly on a walk with me after supper and there he lets himself be analyzed, " and "Eitingon who has picked me up twice weekly for an evening walk on which he lets himself be analyzed, comes Friday for a last time and then goes to Berlin for a year." Early training analyses, according to Balint, were short and informal. When the dynamics of the unconscious and of transference were demonstrated to the trainee, his further training was left to his personal ingenuity. In a later generation of European psychoanalysts, Balint reports, there was a frequent need for additional analysis after completed official training. This was mostly carried out privately and secretly in order to spare the prestige of the analyst.

I do not want to go deeper into the history of training analysis, Balint (2) and Ekstein (4) have reported this history very aptly. They have shown that training analysis and its termination has been and is full of unsolved problems. The differences and similarities between training and therapeutic analysis have been frequently discussed. Anna Freud (6) has pointed out the similarity between training and child analysis. In child analysis modifications of classical technique were found to be unavoidable. Modifications in the technique of training analysis have been discussed by Alexander (1), Grotjahn (8), and others. Even those psychoanalysts who prefer no modifications of classical technique are fully aware that the reality situation in which training analysis takes place is different from that of a therapeutic analysis.

630

[[RunningHead]]

The Symbolic Meaning of the Elbow11

Earl Loomis M.D.

Earl Loomis

Earl Loomis  

Despite their presence in the average ratio of two per person and their manifest usefulness, elbows are commonly overlooked as psychological symbols. This is not the case with one whose elbow has been immobilized on account of a fracture or a burn and who ever so quickly discovered how important his missed function really was. Humor and slang also honor the elbow with a few signs of recognition: It is called the "funnybone" or "crazybone, " and if one is in a hurry, he or she may "elbow" his or her way through a crowd. "Bending the elbow" is a popular and often pathological pursuit, and its converse, probably equally or more pathological, is seen when elbows of young children are immobilized to prevent thumb sucking. Every anatomist and physician is also all too familiar with the elbow's orthopedic potentials. The elbow is really a remarkable joint, providing for flexion, extension, pronation, and supination, making possible hand-mouth approximation and immeasurably enhancing the range of usefulness of the hand. It is through the elbow that we clasp things to us and throw them away. To "give someone the elbow" is to make a hostile gesture, and to demand elbow room is to ask for space in which to live, work, or play.

This paper does not deal primarily with any of the above aspects of the elbow, although it is related to all of them—rather it consists of a few speculations growing out of a clinical observation.

1Read before the Midwinter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, December, 1953.2Associate Professor of Child Psychiatry, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine, and Director Children's Residential Treatment Service (Western Psychiatric Institute and Clinics).697
